steps.title: Identify the two independent clauses
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses that must be properly connected: (1) “Such interpretations are ultimately speculative,” and (2) “the object's gray coloration may be an incidental effect of radiation, solar wind, or collisions with other objects rather than evidence of its physical makeup.” Both have a subject and verb and can stand alone. The blank must provide punctuation and a connector that properly joins them.

steps.title: Apply the rule for “though” as a conjunctive adverb
steps.content: The word “though” here functions as a transition word (similar to “however”) introducing a contrasting clause. When “though” connects two independent clauses, a comma precedes it, and a semicolon follows to separate the main ideas. This creates the pattern: IC, though; IC. The comma attaches the transition to the first clause, and the semicolon marks the separation before the second independent clause begins. Options A and C omit necessary punctuation; option A treats “though” as a subordinating conjunction (creating a dependent clause), while option C places the semicolon before “though” rather than after. Option D adds an extra comma, creating faulty punctuation. That matches choice B — B is correct.
